questions.text: The work function of a metal is 1 eV. Light of wavelength 3000 Å is incident on this metal surface. The velocity of the emitted photo-electrons will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As it is a calculation-based question, it only has one correct option, which is D.
As it is a calculation-based question, it only has one correct option, which is D.
As it is a calculation-based question, it only has one correct option, which is D.

Convert the wavelength to meters: 3000 Å = 3000 x 10-10 m = 3 x 10-7 mCalculate the energy of the incident photon (E) using the formula E = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant (6.626 x 10-34 Js) and c is the speed of light (3 x 108 m/s). E = (6.626 x 10-34 Js * 3 x 108 m/s) / (3 x 10-7 m) = 6.626 x 10-19 J Convert the work function (Φ) from eV to Joules: 1 eV = 1.602 x 10-19 J Calculate the kinetic energy (KE) of the emitted electron using KE = E - Φ.KE = (6.626 x 10-19 J) - (1.602 x 10-19 J) = 5.024 x 10-19 JCalculate the velocity (v) of the electron using KE = 1/2 mv2, where m is the mass of an electron (9.109 x 10-31 kg).v = √(2KE/m) = √((2 * 5.024 x 10-19 J) / (9.109 x 10-31 kg)) ≈ 1.05 x 106 m/s

questions.explanation: Convert the wavelength to meters: 3000 Å = 3000 x 10-10 m = 3 x 10-7 m Calculate the energy of the incident photon (E) using the formula E = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant (6.626 x 10-34 Js) and c is the speed of light (3 x 108 m/s). E = (6.626 x 10-34 Js * 3 x 108 m/s) / (3 x 10-7 m) = 6.626 x 10-19 J Convert the work function (Φ) from eV to Joules: 1 eV = 1.602 x 10-19 JCalculate the kinetic energy (KE) of the emitted electron using KE = E - Φ. KE = (6.626 x 10-19 J) - (1.602 x 10-19 J) = 5.024 x 10-19 JCalculate the velocity (v) of the electron using KE = 1/2 mv2, where m is the mass of an electron (9.109 x 10-31 kg). v = √(2KE/m) = √((2 * 5.024 x 10-19 J) / (9.109 x 10-31 kg)) ≈ 1.05 x 106m/s

questions.text: A material whose K absorption edge is 0.15 Å is irradiated with 0.1 Å X-rays. The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons that are emitted from the K-shell is :
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct. This is the calculated maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons using the difference in energy between the incident X-rays and the K absorption edge.To determine the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, we need to calculate the energy of the incident X-rays and the energy corresponding to the K absorption edge. The energy of a photon is given by E = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant and c is the speed of light.The energy of the 0.1 Å X-rays is calculated as:Eincident = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.1 x 10-10 m) = 1.986 x 10-15 J = 124 keVThe energy corresponding to the K absorption edge is:EK = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.15 x 10-10 m) = 1.324 x 10-15 J = 83 keVThe maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is the difference:Ekinetic = Eincident - EK = 124 keV - 83 keV = 41 keVThus, the correct answer is 41 keV. The other options represent incorrect calculations of this energy difference, leading to overestimated or inconsistent values.
Incorrect. This value overestimates the energy difference, leading to an incorrect calculation of kinetic energy.
Incorrect. This value is not consistent with the energy difference based on the given wavelengths.
Incorrect. This value greatly overestimates the kinetic energy and does not match the calculated energy difference.

questions.explanation: To determine the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, we need to calculate the energy of the incident X-rays and the energy corresponding to the K absorption edge. The energy of a photon is given by E = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant and c is the speed of light.The energy of the 0.1 Å X-rays is calculated as:Eincident = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.1 x 10-10 m) = 1.986 x 10-15 J = 124 keVThe energy corresponding to the K absorption edge is:EK = (6.626 x 10-34 J·s)(3 x 108 m/s) / (0.15 x 10-10 m) = 1.324 x 10-15 J = 83 keVThe maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is the difference:Ekinetic = Eincident - EK = 124 keV - 83 keV = 41 keVThus, the correct answer is 41 keV. The other options represent incorrect calculations of this energy difference, leading to overestimated or inconsistent values.

questions.text: In an experiment, a tungsten cathode that has a threshold wavelength of 2300 Å is irradiated by ultraviolet light of wavelength 1800 Å. The maximum energy of emitted photo-electrons will be:

options.explanation: The energy of the incoming light (shorter wavelength) is not low enough to give such a small kinetic energy.1.2 eV is too small compared to what we expect when the difference between the incident wavelength (1800 Å) and threshold wavelength (2300 Å) is significant.

The light used (1800 Å) is more energetic than the minimum required (2300 Å).The difference in their energies is moderate, so the emitted electrons will have a moderate, not too small and not too large kinetic energy.1.5 eV fits exactly with the expected amount of extra energy.
This is only slightly above the correct answer.But the wavelength difference between 1800 Å and 2300 Å is not large enough to give more than 1.5 eV.So 1.6 eV is slightly too high.

questions.text: Stopping potentials of 24, 100, 110, 115 kV are measured for photoelectrons emitted from a certain element when it is irradiated with monochromatic X-rays. The element is used as a target in an X-ray tube. The energy of the K line is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: (100−24)×103 eV = 76×103 eVHence, this option is incorrect according to the solution.

Stopping potentials are 24, 100, 110 and 115 kV, i.e. if the electrons are emitted from conduction band maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons would be 115×103 eV. If they are emitted from next inner shell, maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons would be 110×103 eV and so on. for photoelectrons of L- shell it would be 100×103 eV and for K- shell it is 24×103 ev. Therefore, diference between energy of L-shell and K-shell isΔE=EL−KK=(100−24)×103 eV=76×103eV=76KevHence, this option is correct according to the solution.
(100−24)×103 eV = 76×103 eVHence, this option is incorrect according to the solution.

questions.text: All electrons ejected from a surface by incident light of wavelength 200 nm can be stopped before travelling 1 m in the direction of a uniform electric field of 4N/C. The work function of the surface is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: 4 eV is the maximum kinetic energy that the field can remove (not the work function). If the work function were 4 eV, the photon (6.2 eV) would give at most 2.2 eV KE — which contradicts the stopping condition. So this option confuses KE with work function.
6.2 eV is the photon energy itself (from 200 nm). If the work function were 6.2 eV, no electrons would be emitted. That contradicts the premise that electrons are ejected and then stopped within 1 m.

2.0 eV is too small. If the work function were 2.0 eV, the maximum kinetic energy would be 6.2−2.0=4.2 eV, which is greater than the 4 eV the field can stop — some electrons would travel beyond 1 m. That contradicts the statement that all electrons are stopped before 1 m.

This value makes the maximum possible kinetic energy 6.2−2.2=4.0 eV, exactly the energy the field (4 V over 1 m) can remove. Thus all emitted electrons can be stopped within 1 m, matching the experiment.

questions.explanation: This value makes the maximum possible kinetic energy 6.2−2.2=4.0 eV, exactly the energy the field (4 V over 1 m) can remove. Thus all emitted electrons can be stopped within 1 m, matching the experiment.This is the following solution:

questions.text: A cesium photocell, with a steady potential difference of 60V across it, is illuminated by a small bright light placed 1 m away. When the same light is placed 2m away, the number of electrons crossing the photocell:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Each carries one-quarter of their previous momentum
options.explanation: The electrons are a quarter as numerous as the intensity decreases by 1/4 (refer to the explanation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Each carries one-quarter of their previous energy
options.explanation: The electrons are a quarter as numerous as the intensity decreases by 1/4 (refer to the explanation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.

The intensity of light is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the light source and the photocell. When the light source is moved from 1 m to 2 m away, the intensity of light is reduced to 1/4 of the original intensity. This means that the number of electrons emitted by the photocell is also reduced to 1/4 of the original number.
The electrons are a quarter as numerous as the intensity decreases by 1/4 (refer to the explanation below). Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The intensity of light is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the light source and the photocell. When the light source is moved from 1 m to 2 m away, the intensity of light is reduced to 1/4 of the original intensity. This means that the number of electrons emitted by the photocell is also reduced to 1/4 of the original number.

questions.text: The kinetic energy of most energetic electrons emitted from a metallic surface is doubled when the wavelength of the incident radiation is changed from 400 nm to 310 nm. The work function of the metal is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As per the calculation, the correct answer is 2.2 ev. Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per the calculation (refer to the explanation below), the correct answer is 2.2 ev. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Let's first calculate the energy of the first radiation.Energy = hv/λ = (6.63x10-34 x 3x108)/ 400x10-9 = 4.9725x10-19 J = 4.9725x10-19 / 1.6 x10-19 = 3.1 evThe energy of the second radiationEnergy = hv/λ = (6.63x10-34 x 3x108)/ 310x10-9 = 6.416x10-19 J= 6.416x10-19/1.6x10-19= 4 evK.E = Energy - Φ = 3.1 - ΦThe second radiation will have twice K.E2K.E = 4 - ΦK.E = (4 - Φ)/2K.E = K.E(4 - Φ) / 2 = 3.1 - Φ4 - Φ = 2(3.1 - Φ)4 - Φ = 6.2 - 2Φ-Φ + 2Φ = 6.2 - 4Φ = 2.2 ev
As per the calculation, the correct answer is 2.2 ev. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Let's first calculate the energy of the first radiation.Energy = hv/λ = (6.63x10-34 x 3x108)/ 400x10-9 = 4.9725x10-19 J = 4.9725x10-19 / 1.6 x10-19 = 3.1 evThe energy of the second radiationEnergy = hv/λ = (6.63x10-34 x 3x108)/ 310x10-9 = 6.416x10-19 J= 6.416x10-19/1.6x10-19= 4 evK.E = Energy - Φ = 3.1 - ΦThe second radiation will have twice K.E2K.E = 4 - ΦK.E = (4 - Φ)/2K.E = K.E(4 - Φ) / 2 = 3.1 - Φ4 - Φ = 2(3.1 - Φ)4 - Φ = 6.2 - 2Φ-Φ + 2Φ = 6.2 - 4Φ = 2.2 ev

questions.text: Two identical metal plates show photoelectric effect. Light of wavelength λA falls on plate A and λ B falls on plate B. λA = 2λ B. The maximum K.E. of the photoelectrons are KA and KB respectively. Which one of the following is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option states that 2 times the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate A is equal to the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate B.Since the frequency of light is inversely proportional to its wavelength, the energy of photons is directly proportional to their frequency. Therefore, if λA = 2λB, the frequency of light for plate A will be half of the frequency for plate B. Consequently, the maximum kinetic energy for plate B should be higher than that for plate A. Thus, 2KA cannot be equal to KB

This option states that the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate A is equal to 2 times the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate B.Using the same logic as in option A, we can conclude that KA cannot be equal to 2KB.

This option states that the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate A is less than half of the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate B.Since the frequency of light is directly proportional to the energy of photons, and λA= 2λB, the frequency of light for plate A will be twice the frequency for plate B. This means that the energy of the photons incident on plate A will be higher than that on plate B, resulting in a higher maximum kinetic energy for plate A. Therefore, KA is indeed less than KB/2.

This option states that the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate A is greater than 2 times the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons on plate B.Using the same logic as in option C, we can conclude that KA cannot be greater than 2KB.

questions.text: The human eye can barely detect a yellow light (6000 Å) that delivers 1.7 × 10–18 watt to the retina. Nearly how many photons per second does the retina receive?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Using the same calculation process as in option b, we would find a different result.Therefore, this option is incorrect.

To calculate the number of photons per second, we can use the formula:Number of photons = (Power) / (Energy per photon)The energy per photon can be calculated using the equation:Energy per photon = (hc) / λUsing the given formula:Number of photons = (1.7 × 10-18 watt) / [(6.626 × 10-34 J⋅s × 3 × 108 m/s) / (6000 × 10-10 m)]Simplifying the expression:Number of photons = (1.7 × 10-18watt) / [(6.626 × 3) / 6000]Number of photons ≈ 5 photons per second, we would find the result to be approximately 5 photons per second. Therefore, this option is correct.

questions.text: When a metallic surface is illuminated with monochromatic light of wavelength λ, the stopping potential for photoelectric current is 3V0. When the same metallic surface is illuminated with a light of wavelength 2λ, the stopping potential is V0. The threshold wavelength for the surface is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: ubstituting the values into the equation:3V0 = (hc / e) * (1 / λ) - (φ / e)V0 = (hc / e) * (1 / 6λ) - (φ / e)Dividing both sides by 3:V0/ 3 = (hc / e) * (1 / (6λ))2V0 / 6 = (hc / e) * (1 / λ)V0 / 3 = (hc / e) * (1 / λ)This equation contradicts the given information, so option A is not correct.

The relationship between the stopping potential and the threshold wavelength can be expressed as:Stopping potential = (hc / e) * (1 / λ) - (φ / e)Substituting the values into the equation:3V0 = (hc / e) * (1 / λ) - (φ / e)V0 = (hc / e) * (1 / 4λ) - (φ / e)Dividing both sides by 2:V0 / 2 = (hc / e) * (1 / (4λ))V0 / 2 = (hc / e) * (1 / 2λ)V0 / 2 = (hc / e) * (1 / λ)This equation matches the given information, so option B is correct.

Substituting the values into the equation:3V0= (hc / e) * (1 / λ) - (φ / e)V0= (hc / e) * (1 / (4λ/3)) - (φ / e)V0 = (hc / e) * (3 / (4λ))Dividing both sides by 3:V0 / 3 = (hc / e) * (1 / (4λ))This equation contradicts the given information, so option C is not correct.

Substituting the values into the equation:3V0= (hc / e) * (1 / λ) - (φ / e)V0 = (hc / e) * (1 / (8λ)) - (φ / e)Dividing both sides by 3:V0 / 3 = (hc / e) * (1 / (8λ))This equation contradicts the given information, so option D is not correct.

questions.text: A metallic surface ejects photoelectrons when hit by green light but none when hit by yellow light. Will the electrons be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option states that electrons will be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light. However, based on the information given, the surface only ejects photoelectrons when hit by green light and does not eject photoelectrons when hit by yellow light. There is no direct information about the behavior of the surface when hit by red light. Therefore, it is not certain whether electrons will be ejected when the surface is hit by red light. This option is not necessarily true.

options.text: Yes, if the red beam is sufficiently intense
options.explanation: This option suggests that electrons will be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light, but the intensity of the red light needs to be sufficiently high. The intensity of light affects the number of photons incident on the surface and, therefore, the number of ejected photoelectrons. If the red beam is intense enough, it can provide sufficient energy to eject photoelectrons from the metallic surface. Therefore, this option is plausible.

options.text: Yes, if the red beam is allowed to fall for sufficient duration
options.explanation: This option suggests that electrons will be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light, but the duration of exposure to the red light needs to be sufficient. The duration of exposure determines the total energy transferred to the surface, which can lead to the ejection of photoelectrons. If the red beam falls on the surface for a sufficient period, the surface can absorb enough energy to eject photoelectrons. Therefore, this option is also plausible.

This option states that electrons will not be ejected if the same surface is hit by red light. Based on the given information that the surface ejects photoelectrons when hit by green light but none when hit by yellow light, we can infer that the work function of the metallic surface is higher than the energy of yellow light but lower than the energy of green light. If the red light has lower energy than green light, it is unlikely to have sufficient energy to overcome the work function and eject photoelectrons. Therefore, this option is likely the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Photons are electrically neutral, therefore, are not deflected by magnetic or electric field. Rest mass of photon is zero because light always travel with some speed and is never at rest.

questions.text: Quantum nature of light is explained by which of the following phenomenon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Huygens proposed that every point that a luminous disturbance meets turns into a source of the spherical wave itself. The sum of the secondary waves, which are the result of the disturbance, determines what form the new wave will take. This theory of light is known as the 'Huygens' Principle'.

According to quantum theory, light is in the form of packets of energy called photons. The energy of the photon is equal to hf. Here, h is the Planck's constant and f is the frequency of the photon.The emission of electrons when light is shone onto a substance is known as the photoelectric effect. These electrons are referred to as photo-electrons.Based on quantum theory, in the photoelectric effect only discrete amounts of energy, known as quanta, can be absorbed or released by electrons (packets).

Maxwell discovered that electric and magnetic fields travel through space moving at the same speed of light as waves. Electromagnetic theory: He stated that light is a propagating wave of electric and magnetic field. The theory describes the interaction between the electric field and magnetic field.

According to De Broglie's theory of matter waves, each particle of matter with linear momentum is also a wave. The amount of a particle's linear momentum is inversely proportional to the wavelength of a matter wave associated with that particle.

questions.explanation: The photoelectric effect is the process in which electromagnetic radiation ejects electrons from a material. Einstein proposed photons to be quanta of electromagnetic radiation having energy E=hν is the frequency of the radiation. All electromagnetic radiation is composed of photons.

Therefore, the intensity of the light will remain the same.As a result, the photoelectric current will also remain the same. i.e., I.

options.explanation: Diffraction shows how light can bend and interfere like a wave, while the photoelectric effect reveals that light also behaves as discrete packets of energy—photons. Together they illustrate the wave‑particle duality that is fundamental to quantum theory.

questions.text: The mass of a photon at rest is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.
It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

The rest mass of photon is zero. The mass of any particle at rest (relative to the observer) is known as the rest mass of that particle. One of the consequences of the Einstein's special theory of relativity is that the mass of the particle increases with increasing velocity relative to the observer. Since the photons are bound to travel at the speed of light (again a consequence of Einstein's special theory of relativity), a non-zero rest mass would lead to an infinitely massive particle.

questions.text: Let nr and nb be respectively the number of photons emitted by a red bulb and blue bulb of equal power in a given time. Then:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.
It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

The number of photons emitted by the red bulb is greater than the number of photons emitted by the blue bulb.Here is a mathematical explanation:Let the wavelength of the red light be λr and the wavelength of the blue light be λbThe number of photons emitted by the red bulb is given by the following equation:n r = E/hν = E/h(c/λr) = Eλr/hcThe number of photons emitted by the blue bulb is given by the following equation:n b = E/hν = E/h(c/λb) = Eλb/hcSince the power of the red bulb is equal to the power of the blue bulb, we have:nr= nb Substituting the equations for nr and nb , we get:Eλr/hc = Eλb/hcThis means that:λr/λb= 1This means that the wavelength of the red light is equal to the wavelength of the blue light. However, the frequency of the red light is greater than the frequency of the blue light. This is because the speed of light is the same for all wavelengths. Since the frequency of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, the number of photons emitted by the red bulb is greater than the number of photons emitted by the blue bulb.

options.text: The information is insufficient to get a relation between nr and nb
options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The number of photons emitted by the red bulb is greater than the number of photons emitted by the blue bulb.Here is a mathematical explanation:Let the wavelength of the red light be λr and the wavelength of the blue light be λb .The number of photons emitted by the red bulb is given by the following equation:n r = E/hν = E/h(c/λr) = Eλr/hcThe number of photons emitted by the blue bulb is given by the following equation:n b = E/hν = E/h(c/λb) = Eλb/hcSince the power of the red bulb is equal to the power of the blue bulb, we have:n r = n b Substituting the equations for n r and n b , we get:Eλr/hc = Eλb/hc This means that:λr/λb = 1This means that the wavelength of the red light is equal to the wavelength of the blue light. However, the frequency of the red light is greater than the frequency of the blue light. This is because the speed of light is the same for all wavelengths. Since the frequency of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, the number of photons emitted by the red bulb is greater than the number of photons emitted by the blue bulb.

questions.text: λ is proportional to?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

options.text: 1/√ E for both photons and particles
options.explanation: It is incorrect as per the given explanation.

options.text: 1/E for photons and 1/√ E for particles
options.explanation: The wavelength of a photon is inversely proportional to its energy, but for particles, the wavelength is inversely proportional to the square root of its energy. This is because the energy of a photon is given by the following equation: E = hν where: h is Planck's constant ν is the frequency of the photon The frequency of a photon is given by the following equation: ν = c/λ where: c is the speed of light λ is the wavelength of the photon Substituting the equation for ν into the equation for E, we get: E = h(c/λ) This means that E is inversely proportional to λ. For particles, the wavelength is given by the following equation: λ = h/p where: h is Planck's constant p is the momentum of the particle The momentum of a particle is given by the following equation: p = mv where: m is the mass of the particle v is the velocity of the particle Substituting the equation for p into the equation for λ, we get: λ = h/mv This means that λ is inversely proportional to the square root of E.

This is because the energy of a photon is given by the following equation: E = hν where: h is Planck's constant ν is the frequency of the photon The frequency of a photon is given by the following equation: ν = c/λ where: c is the speed of light λ is the wavelength of the photon Substituting the equation for ν into the equation for E, we get: E = h(c/λ) This means that E is inversely proportional to λ. For particles, the wavelength is given by the following equation: λ = h/p where: h is Planck's constant p is the momentum of the particle The momentum of a particle is given by the following equation: p = mv where: m is the mass of the particle v is the velocity of the particle Substituting the equation for p into the equation for λ, we get: λ = h/mv This means that λ is inversely proportional to the square root of E.

questions.text: Which of the following graphs correcty represents the variation of particle momentum with associated de broglie wavelength?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is incorrect according to the solution.
It is incorrect according to the solution.
It is incorrect according to the solution.

The inversely proportional graph correctly represents the variation of particle momentum with associated de Broglie wavelength. The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by the following equation:λ = h/p where:λ is the de Broglie wavelengthh is Planck's constantp is the momentum of the particleThis means that the wavelength is inversely proportional to the momentum. In other words, as the momentum of the particle increases, the wavelength decreases. The graph of momentum versus wavelength is therefore an inverse graph.Here is an example of an inverse graph of momentum versus wavelength:Momentum (p) is inversly proportional to Wavelength (λ)10 p | 1/10 λ20 p | 1/20 λ30 p | 1/30 λAs you can see, as the momentum of the particle increases, the wavelength decreases. The inverse relationship between momentum and wavelength is a fundamental property of quantum mechanics. It is responsible for the wave-particle duality of matter, which is the property of matter to exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.

questions.text: A photon is considered to have?

options.explanation: A photon is considered to have energy. The energy of a photon is given by the following equation:E = hν where:h is Planck's constantν is the frequency of the photon

Yes, a photon is considered to have both wavelength and frequency. This is because a photon is a quantum of light, and light is a wave. The wavelength of a photon is the distance between two consecutive peaks of the wave, and the frequency of a photon is the number of waves that pass a point in a given time. The wavelength of a photon is given by the following equation:λ = h/p where:λ is the wavelength of the photonh is Planck's constantp is the momentum of the photonThe frequency of a photon is given by the following equation:ν = c/λ where:ν is the frequency of the photon As you can see, the wavelength and frequency of a photon are inversely proportional. This means that as the wavelength of a photon increases, the frequency decreases, and vice versa.

Yes, a photon is considered to have momentum. This is because photons carry energy, and energy is equivalent to momentum. The momentum of a photon is given by the following equation: p = h/λ where:p is the momentum of the photonh is Planck's constantλ is the wavelength of the photonThe momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength. This means that photons with shorter wavelengths have higher momenta.

This means that as the wavelength of a photon increases, the frequency decreases, and vice versa.A photon is considered to have momentum. This is because photons carry energy, and energy is equivalent to momentum. The momentum of a photon is given by the following equation:p = h/λ where:p is the momentum of the photonh is Planck's constantλ is the wavelength of the photonThe momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength. This means that photons with shorter wavelengths have higher momenta.

questions.text: If electron and proton have same de broglie wavelength, which have greater speed?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The electron is much lighter than the proton (approximately 1836 times lighter), for them to have the same momentum, the electron must have a greater velocity (speed) compared to the proton. This is because, for the same momentum, a lighter particle needs to move faster than a heavier particle. Since, electron has the least mass. So electron will possess highest velocity.
Proton is heavier than the electron thus, its speed is lesser as compared to electron.

If an electron and a proton have the same de Broglie wavelength, it implies that they have the same momentum.However, the momentum of a particle is related to its speed. The momentum (p) of a particle is given by the equation p = m * v, where m is the mass of the particle and v is its velocity.

options.text: Electron and Proton cannot have wavelength
options.explanation: Electrons and protons can have wavelengths. This is because of the wave-particle duality of matter, which is the property of matter to exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.

questions.text: The wavelength of a moving particle is inversely proportional to?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: No, the wavelength of a moving particle is inversely proportional to the particle's momentum, not its mass. The de Broglie wavelength of a moving particle is given by the following equation: λ = h / p where: λ is the de Broglie wavelength h is Planck's constant p is the momentum of the particle

The wavelength of a moving particle is inversely proportional to its velocity. This means that particles with higher velocities have shorter wavelengths.
